title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the piano. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Hammer and the String: Evolution of the Piano
content.text: For over two centuries, the piano has been the centerpiece of Western music, serving as both a versatile solo instrument and a vital part of the orchestra. Yet, its invention was born out of a specific technical frustration. In the early 1700s, keyboard players were limited by the harpsichord, an instrument whose strings were plucked, meaning it produced the same volume regardless of how hard the keys were pressed. ||(41)|| [41]Cristofori's revolutionary idea was to replace the plucking mechanism with a leather-covered hammer that struck the strings and then immediately recoiled[/41]. This allowed the player to vary the volume and tone through the force of their touch. He called his invention the 'gravicembalo col piano e forte'—literally, the 'harpsichord with soft and loud.' ||(42)|| [42]Initially, the musical world was slow to embrace the change[/42]. Early pianos were expensive, temperamental, and lacked the bright, cutting sound of the harpsichord that audiences were used to. However, the instrument found a champion in the German builder Gottfried Silbermann, whose designs eventually caught the attention of a young Johann Sebastian Bach. ||(43)|| [43]As the Industrial Revolution took hold, the piano underwent a series of rapid transformations[/43]. The wooden frames of the early models were prone to warping under the immense tension of the strings. To solve this, engineers introduced the cast-iron frame, which allowed for thicker strings and a much more powerful, resonant sound. ||(44)|| [44]This newfound power matched the spirit of the Romantic era[/44]. Composers like Franz Liszt and Frédéric Chopin pushed the instrument to its physical limits, demanding a range of expression and volume that would have been unthinkable on Cristofori’s original model. The piano became a symbol of middle-class aspiration, with a keyboard in almost every respectable Victorian parlor. ||(45)|| [45]However, the 20th century brought the most radical departure yet[/45]. The birth of electronic music and the invention of the digital synthesizer challenged the piano’s dominance. Musicians began to experiment with 'prepared pianos,' placing coins, bolts, or rubber between the strings to create percussive, otherworldly sounds. ||(46)|| [46]Despite the rise of digital alternatives, the acoustic grand piano remains the gold standard for concert halls and conservatories[/46]. Its complex mechanical action and the natural vibration of its wooden soundboard create a depth of tone that silicon chips have yet to perfectly replicate. The piano is not just a machine; it is a living history of our quest for musical expression.

paragraphs.A: This [41] lack of dynamic control [/41] was finally addressed by Bartolomeo Cristofori, a harpsichord maker in Florence. He realized that for keyboard music to truly express emotion, it needed to mimic the nuances of the human voice and the violin.
paragraphs.B: This [42] cumbersome name was eventually shortened [/42] to 'pianoforte' and finally just 'piano.' While the name grew simpler, the internal mechanics became increasingly complex as other builders sought to improve on the original design.
paragraphs.F: Although [43] Bach was initially critical [/43] of the instrument’s weak high notes, he later praised the improved versions. This endorsement from a master musician helped legitimize the piano, sparking a wave of innovation across Europe that would lead to the rise of the great piano manufacturers.
paragraphs.E: These [44] structural reinforcements [/44] were accompanied by the invention of the 'double escapement' action. This allowed a note to be repeated rapidly without the key having to return to its original position, a feature that paved the way for the virtuosic compositions of the 19th century.
paragraphs.D: The [45] ubiquity of the instrument [/45] also fueled a massive publishing industry for sheet music. Arrangements of symphonies and operas allowed families to enjoy the latest musical hits in their own homes, long before the invention of the phonograph or the radio.
paragraphs.G: This [46] era of avant-garde experimentation [/46] proved that the piano’s potential was far from exhausted. It showed that the instrument could adapt to the changing sounds of the modern world, even if it meant distorting the very tones it was originally designed to produce.
paragraphs.C: Many [47] modern upright pianos [/47] are designed to save space in small apartments. While they use the same basic hammer-and-string mechanism, their vertical strings and smaller soundboards mean they can never achieve the same projection as a full-sized concert grand.

questions.explanation: A refers back to the 'lack of dynamic control' (same volume regardless of touch) mentioned in the harpsichord description and introduces Cristofori.
B explains how the long name 'gravicembalo col piano e forte' was shortened to 'piano', providing a direct linguistic link to the previous paragraph.
F follows the mention of Bach and provides his specific reaction and endorsement, which 'legitimized the piano' as the text continues.
E refers to 'structural reinforcements' (the cast-iron frame) and adds more technical details about the 'double escapement' action.
D expands on the 'ubiquity' of the piano in Victorian parlors, explaining how it fueled the sheet music industry.
G refers to the 'era of avant-garde experimentation' (prepared pianos, bolts, and rubber) mentioned before the gap.
